problem:
Let $\mathcal R$ be an [[Definition:Equivalence Relation|equivalence relation]] on a [[Definition:Set|set]] $S$.\nThen every [[Definition:Element|element]] of $S$ is in its own [[Definition:Equivalence Class|$\mathcal R$-class]]: 
 :$\forall x \in S: x \in \eqclass x {\mathcal R}$

Let $G$ be a $	uple {p, q}$-[[Definition:Undirected Graph|undirected graph]], which may be a [[Definition:Multigraph|multigraph]] or a [[Definition:Loop-Graph|loop-graph]], or both.
Let $V = \set {v_1, v_2, \ldots, v_p}$ be the [[Definition:Vertex Set|vertex set]] of $G$.
Then: 
 :$\displaystyle \sum_{i \mathop = 1}^p \map {\deg_G} {v_i} = 2 q$ 
 where $\map {\deg_G} {v_i}$ is the [[Definition:Degree of Vertex|degree]] of [[Definition:Vertex of Graph|vertex]] $v_i$.
That is, the [[Definition:Integer Addition|sum]] of all the [[Definition:Degree of Vertex|degrees]] of all the [[Definition:Vertex of Graph|vertices]] of an [[Definition:Undirected Graph|graph]] is equal to twice its [[Definition:Size of Graph|size]].
This result is known as the '''Handshake Lemma''' or '''Handshaking Lemma'''.
solution_steps:
In the [[Definition:Graph Notation|notation]] $	uple {p, q}$-[[Definition:Graph (Graph Theory)|graph]], $p$ is its [[Definition:Order of Graph|order]] and $q$ its [[Definition:Size of Graph|size]].
That is, $p$ is the number of [[Definition:Vertex of Graph|vertices]] in $G$, and $q$ is the number of [[Definition:Edge of Graph|edges]] in $G$.
Each [[Definition:Edge of Graph|edge]] is [[Definition:Incident (Undirected Graph)|incident]] to exactly two [[Definition:Vertex of Graph|vertices]].
The [[Definition:Degree of Vertex|degree]] of each [[Definition:Vertex of Graph|vertex]] is defined as the number of [[Definition:Edge of Graph|edges]] to which it is [[Definition:Incident (Undirected Graph)|incident]].
So when we add up the [[Definition:Degree of Vertex|degrees]] of all the [[Definition:Vertex of Graph|vertices]], we are counting all the [[Definition:Edge of Graph|edges]] of the graph twice.
{{qed}}

problem:
Let $\RR$ be an [[Definition:Equivalence Relation|equivalence relation]] on a [[Definition:Set|set]] $S$.\nThen: 
 :$\tuple {x, y} \in \RR \iff \eqclass x \RR = \eqclass y \RR$

Let $\RR$ be an [[Definition:Equivalence Relation|equivalence relation]] on a [[Definition:Set|set]] $S$.\nThen all [[Definition:Equivalence Class|$\RR$-classes]] are [[Definition:Pairwise Disjoint|pairwise disjoint]]: 
 :$\tuple {x, y} \notin \RR \iff \eqclass x \RR \cap \eqclass y \RR = \O$

Let $\RR \subseteq S \times S$ be an [[Definition:Equivalence Relation|equivalence]] on a [[Definition:Set|set]] $S$.\nThen the [[Definition:Quotient Set|quotient]] $S / \RR$ of $S$ by $\RR$ forms a [[Definition:Set Partition|partition]] of $S$.
solution_steps:
To prove that $S / \RR$ is a [[Definition:Set Partition|partition]] of $S$, we have to prove: 
 :$(1): \quad \displaystyle  igcup {S / \RR} = S$ 
 :$(2): \quad \eqclass x \RR 
e \eqclass y \RR \iff \eqclass x \RR \cap \eqclass y \RR = \O$ 
 :$(3): \quad  orall \eqclass x \RR \in S / \RR: \eqclass x \RR 
e \O$
Taking each proposition in turn:
=== [[Union of Equivalence Classes is Whole Set]] ===
The set of [[Definition:Equivalence Class|$\RR$-classes]] constitutes the whole of $S$: 
 {{:Union of Equivalence Classes is Whole Set}}{{qed|lemma}}
=== [[Equivalence Classes are Disjoint]] === 
 {{:Equivalence Classes are Disjoint/Proof 1}}{{qed|lemma}}
=== [[Equivalence Class is not Empty]] === 
 {{:Equivalence Class is not Empty}}{{qed|lemma}}
Thus all conditions for $S / \RR$ to be a [[Definition:Set Partition|partition]] are fulfilled.
{{qed}}

problem: Let $\mathcal R$ be an [[Definition:Equivalence Relation|equivalence relation]] on $S$.\nFor each $x \in S$, the [[Definition:Exactly One|one and only one]] [[Definition:Equivalence Class|$\mathcal R$-class]] to which $x$ belongs is $\eqclass x {\mathcal R}$.
solution_steps: This follows directly from the [[Fundamental Theorem on Equivalence Relations]]: the [[Definition:Quotient Set|set]] of $\mathcal R$-classes forms a [[Definition:Set Partition|partition]] of $S$.\n{{qed}}

problem:
Let $\mathcal R$ be an [[Definition:Equivalence Relation|equivalence]] on $S$. 
 {{refactor|Use TFAE template}}\nThen $\forall x, y \in S$, the following statements are all equivalent: 
 :$(1): \quad x$ and $y$ are in the same [[Definition:Equivalence Class|$\mathcal R$-class]] 
 :$(2): \quad \eqclass x {\mathcal R} = \eqclass y {\mathcal R}$ 
 :$(3): \quad x \mathrel {\mathcal R} y$ 
 :$(4): \quad x \in \eqclass y {\mathcal R}$ 
 :$(5): \quad y \in \eqclass x {\mathcal R}$ 
 :$(6): \quad \eqclass x {\mathcal R} \cap \eqclass y {\mathcal R} \ne \O$
solution_steps: : $(1)$ and $(2)$ are equivalent because, by [[Equivalence Class is Unique]], $\eqclass x {\mathcal R}$ is the unique [[Definition:Equivalence Class|$\mathcal R$-class]] to which $x$ belongs, and $\eqclass y {\mathcal R}$ is the unique [[Definition:Equivalence Class|$\mathcal R$-class]] to which $y$ belongs.

As these are unique for each, they must therefore be the same set. 
 : $(2)$ is equivalent to $(3)$ by [[Equivalence Class holds Equivalent Elements]]. 
 : $(3)$ is equivalent to $(4)$ by the definition of [[Definition:Equivalence Class|Equivalence Class]]. 
 : $(3)$ is equivalent to $(5)$ through dint of the [[Definition:Symmetric Relation|symmetry]] of $\mathcal R$ and the definition of [[Definition:Equivalence Class|Equivalence Class]]. 
 : $(3)$ is equivalent to $(6)$ from [[Equivalence Classes are Disjoint]].\n{{qed}}

problem:
Let $\mathbb S$ be a [[Definition:Set Partition|partition]] of a [[Definition:Set|set]] $S$.\nLet $\RR$ be the [[Definition:Relation Induced by Partition|relation induced by $\mathbb S$]].\nThen: 
 :$(1): \quad \RR$ is [[Definition:Unique|unique]] 
 :$(2): \quad \RR$ is an [[Definition:Equivalence Relation|equivalence relation]] on $S$.\nHence $\mathbb S$ is the [[Definition:Quotient Set|quotient set]] of $S$ by $\RR$, that is: 
 :$\mathbb S = S / \RR$

Let $\RR$ be a [[Definition:Relation|relation]] on a [[Definition:Set|set]] $S$.\nThen $S$ can be [[Definition:Set Partition|partitioned]] into [[Definition:Subset|subsets]] by $\RR$ {{iff}} $\RR$ is an [[Definition:Equivalence Relation|equivalence relation]] on $S$.\nThe [[Definition:Set Partition|partition]] of $S$ defined by $\RR$ is the [[Definition:Quotient Set|quotient set]] $S / \RR$.
The [[Definition:Set Intersection|intersection]] of two [[Definition:Equivalence Relation|equivalence relations]] is itself an [[Definition:Equivalence Relation|equivalence relation]].
solution_steps:
Let $\mathcal R_1$ and $\mathcal R_2$ be [[Definition:Equivalence Relation|equivalence relations]] on $S$.
Let $\mathcal R_3 = \mathcal R_1 \cap \mathcal R_2$.
Checking in turn each of the criteria for [[Definition:Equivalence Relation|equivalence]]:
=== Reflexive === 
 [[Definition:Equivalence Relation|Equivalence relations]] are by definition [[Definition:Reflexive Relation|reflexive]].
So, by [[Intersection of Reflexive Relations is Reflexive]], so is $\mathcal R_3$.
=== Symmetric === 
 [[Definition:Equivalence Relation|Equivalence relations]] are by definition [[Definition:Symmetric Relation|symmetric]].
So, by [[Intersection of Symmetric Relations is Symmetric]], so is $\mathcal R_3$.
=== Transitive === 
 [[Definition:Equivalence Relation|Equivalence relations]] are by definition [[Definition:Transitive Relation|transitive]].
So, by [[Intersection of Transitive Relations is Transitive]], so is $\mathcal R_3$.
Thus $\mathcal R_3$ is an [[Definition:Equivalence Relation|equivalence]].
{{qed}}

problem:
Let $\RR$ be a [[Definition:Endorelation|relation]] on a [[Definition:Set|set]] $S$. 
 {{TFAE|def = Equivalence Relation}}
solution_steps:
=== Definition 1 implies Definition 2 ===
Let $\RR$ be an [[Definition:Equivalence Relation/Definition 1|equivalence relation by definition 1]].
By definition, $\RR$ is [[Definition:Reflexive Relation|reflexive]], [[Definition:Symmetric Relation|symmetric]] and [[Definition:Transitive Relation|transitive]].
From [[Relation Contains Diagonal Relation iff Reflexive]]: 
 :$\Delta_S \subseteq \RR$
From [[Relation equals Inverse iff Symmetric]]: 
 :$\RR^{-1} = \RR$ 
 and so by definition of [[Definition:Set Equality/Definition 2|set equality]]: 
 :$\RR^{-1} \subseteq \RR$
From [[Relation contains Composite with Self iff Transitive]]: 
 :$\RR \circ \RR \subseteq \RR$
From [[Union is Smallest Superset]]: 
 :$\Delta_S \cup \RR^{-1} \cup \RR \circ \RR \subseteq \RR$
Thus $\RR$ is an [[Definition:Equivalence Relation/Definition 2|equivalence relation by definition 2]].
{{qed|lemma}}
=== Definition 2 implies Definition 1 ===
Let $\RR$ be an [[Definition:Equivalence Relation/Definition 2|equivalence relation by definition 2]].
That is: 
 :$\Delta_S \cup \RR^{-1} \cup \RR \circ \RR \subseteq \RR$
By [[Union is Smallest Superset]]: 
 :$(1): \quad \Delta_S \subseteq \RR$ 
 :$(2): \quad \RR^{-1} \subseteq \RR$ 
 :$(3): \quad \RR \circ \RR \subseteq \RR$
From [[Relation Contains Diagonal Relation iff Reflexive]], $(1)$ gives directly that $\RR$ is [[Definition:Reflexive Relation|reflexive]].
From [[Inverse Relation Equal iff Subset]], $(2)$ gives that $\RR^{-1} = \RR$.
So from [[Relation equals Inverse iff Symmetric]] $\RR$ is [[Definition:Symmetric Relation|symmetric]].
From [[Relation contains Composite with Self iff Transitive]], $(3)$ gives that $\RR$ is [[Definition:Transitive Relation|transitive]].
So $\RR$ has been shown to be [[Definition:Reflexive Relation|reflexive]], [[Definition:Symmetric Relation|symmetric]] and [[Definition:Transitive Relation|transitive]].
Thus $\RR$ is an [[Definition:Equivalence Relation/Definition 1|equivalence relation by definition 1]].
{{qed}}

problem:
Let $\RR$ be a [[Definition:Relation|relation]] on $S$.\nThen $\RR$ is an [[Definition:Equivalence Relation|equivalence relation]] on $S$ {{iff}}: 
 :$\Delta_S \subseteq \RR$ 
 and: 
 :$\RR = \RR \circ \RR^{-1}$
solution_steps:
=== Necessary Condition ===
Let $\RR$ be an [[Definition:Equivalence Relation|equivalence relation]].
Then by definition, it is [[Definition:Reflexive Relation|reflexive]], [[Definition:Symmetric Relation|symmetric]] and [[Definition:Transitive Relation|transitive]].
As $\RR$ is [[Definition:Reflexive Relation|reflexive]], we have $\Delta_S \subseteq \RR$ from [[Relation Contains Diagonal Relation iff Reflexive]].
As $\RR$ is [[Definition:Transitive Relation|transitive]]: 
 :$\RR \circ \RR \subseteq \RR$ 
 from [[Relation contains Composite with Self iff Transitive]].
But as $\RR$ is [[Definition:Symmetric Relation|symmetric]]: 
 :$\RR = \RR^{-1}$ 
 from [[Relation equals Inverse iff Symmetric]].
Thus: 
 :$\RR \circ \RR^{-1} \subseteq \RR$
Now we need to show that $\RR \subseteq \RR \circ \RR^{-1}$:
Let $	uple {x, y} \in \RR$.
Then as $\RR$ is [[Definition:Reflexive Relation|reflexive]]: 
 :$	uple {x, y} \in \RR \land 	uple {y, y} \in \RR$ 
 and so: 
 :$	uple {x, y} \in \RR \circ \RR$
As $\RR = \RR^{-1}$, it follows that: 
 :$\RR \subseteq \RR \circ \RR^{-1}$
So it has been shown: 
 :$\Delta_S \subseteq \RR$ and $\RR = \RR \circ \RR^{-1}$
{{qed|lemma}}
=== Sufficient Condition ===
Now, let $\Delta_S \subseteq \RR$ and $\RR = \RR \circ \RR^{-1}$.
From [[Relation Contains Diagonal Relation iff Reflexive]], $\RR$ is [[Definition:Reflexive Relation|reflexive]].
Let $	uple {x, y} \in \RR$.
Then: 
 :$	uple {x, y} \in \RR \circ \RR^{-1}$
So: 
 :$\exists z \in S: 	uple {x, z} \in \RR, 	uple {z, y} \in \RR^{-1}$ 
 and so: 
 :$	uple {y, z} \in \RR$
That is, by definition, $\RR$ is [[Definition:Transitive Relation|transitive]].
As $	uple {x, z} \in \RR$: 
 :$	uple {z, x} \in \RR^{-1}$ 
 and so: 
 :$	uple {y, x} \in \RR$
So it follows that $\RR$ is [[Definition:Symmetric Relation|symmetric]].
Thus $\RR$ is [[Definition:Reflexive Relation|reflexive]], [[Definition:Symmetric Relation|symmetric]] and [[Definition:Transitive Relation|transitive]].
Therefore, by definition, $\RR$ is an [[Definition:Equivalence Relation|equivalence relation]].
{{qed}}

problem:
Let $f: S \to T$ be a [[Definition:Mapping|mapping]].\nThen: 
 :$\exists y \in \Rng f: \tuple {x_1, y} \in f \land \tuple {x_2, y} \in f \iff \map f {x_1} = \map f {x_2}$
Let $f$ be a [[Definition:Mapping|mapping]].\nThen its [[Definition:Inverse of Mapping|inverse]] $f^{-1}$ is a [[Definition:One-to-Many Relation|one-to-many relation]].
solution_steps:
We have that $f$ is a [[Definition:Mapping|mapping]].\nHence $f$ is [[Definition:A Fortiori|a fortiori]] a [[Definition:Many-to-One Relation|many-to-one relation]].\nThen from [[Inverse of Many-to-One Relation is One-to-Many]], $f^{-1}$ is [[Definition:One-to-Many Relation|one-to-many]]. 
 {{Qed}} 
 [[Category:Inverse Relations]] 
 [[Category:Inverse Mappings]] 
 ayh6r7f3eifj9ml49b63dzgczo2bazq

problem:
Two [[Definition:Mapping|mappings]] $f_1: S_1 \to T_1, f_2: S_2 \to T_2$ are equal {{iff}}: 
 :$(1): \quad S_1 = S_2$ 
 :$(2): \quad T_1 = T_2$ 
 :$(3): \quad \forall x \in S_1: \map {f_1} x = \map {f_2} x$
solution_steps: This follows directly from [[Equality of Relations]].\n{{qed}}

problem: A [[Definition:Mapping|mapping]] is a [[Definition:Constant Mapping|constant mapping]] {{iff}} its [[Definition:Image of Mapping|image]] is a [[Definition:Singleton|singleton]].
solution_steps: === Necessary Condition ===\nLet $f_c: S \to T$ be a [[Definition:Constant Mapping|constant mapping]].

problem:
Let $\RR \subseteq S \times T$ be a [[Definition:Relation|relation]] on $S \times T$.\nThen: 
 :$\RR \circ \Delta_S = \RR$ 
 where $\Delta_S$ is the [[Definition:Diagonal Relation|diagonal relation]] on $S$, and $\circ$ signifies [[Definition:Composition of Relations|composition of relations]].
solution_steps:
We use the [[Equality of Relations|definition of relation equality]], as follows:
=== Equality of Codomains ===
The [[Definition:Codomain of Relation|codomains]] of $\RR$ and $\RR \circ \Delta_S$ are both equal to $T$ from [[Codomain of Composite Relation]].
=== Equality of Domains ===
The [[Definition:Domain of Relation|domains]] of $\RR$ and $\RR \circ \Delta_S$ are also easily shown to be equal.
From [[Domain of Composite Relation]]: 
 :$\Dom {\RR \circ \Delta_S} = \Dom {\Delta_S}$
But from the definition of the [[Definition:Diagonal Relation|diagonal relation]]: 
 :$\Dom {\Delta_S} = \Img {\Delta_S} = S$
=== Equality of Relations ===
The [[Definition:Composition of Relations|composite of $\Delta_S$ and $\RR$]] is defined as: 
 :$\RR \circ \Delta_S = \set {	uple {x, z} \in S 	imes T: \exists y \in S: 	uple {x, y} \in \Delta_S \land 	uple {y, z} \in \RR}$
But by definition of the [[Definition:Diagonal Relation|diagonal relation]] on $S$, we have that: 
 :$	uple {x, y} \in \Delta_S \implies x = y$
Hence: 
 :$\RR \circ \Delta_S = \set {	uple {y, z} \in S 	imes T: \exists y \in S: 	uple {y, y} \in \Delta_S \land 	uple {y, z} \in \RR}$
But as $ orall y \in S: 	uple {y, y} \in \Delta_S$, this means: 
 :$\RR \circ \Delta_S = \set {	uple {y, z} \in S 	imes T: 	uple {y, z} \in \RR}$
That is: 
 :$\RR \circ \Delta_S = \RR$
Hence the result.
{{qed}}

problem:
Let $S$ and $T$ be [[Definition:Set|sets]].\nLet $f: S \to T$ be a [[Definition:Mapping|mapping]].\nThen: 
 :$I_T \circ f = f$ 
 where $I_T$ is the [[Definition:Identity Mapping|identity mapping]] on $T$, and $\circ$ signifies [[Definition:Composition of Mappings|composition of mappings]].
solution_steps:
=== Equality of Domains ===
The [[Definition:Domain of Mapping|domains]] of $f$ and $I_T \circ f$ are equal from [[Domain of Composite Relation]]: 
 :$\Dom {I_T \circ f} = \Dom f$
{{qed|lemma}}
=== Equality of Codomains ===
The [[Definition:Codomain of Mapping|codomains]] of $f$ and $f \circ I_S$ are also easily shown to be equal.
From [[Codomain of Composite Relation]], the [[Definition:Codomain of Mapping|codomains]] of $I_T \circ f$ and $I_T$ are both equal to $T$.
But from the definition of the [[Definition:Identity Mapping|identity mapping]], the [[Definition:Codomain of Mapping|codomain]] of $I_T$ is $\Dom {I_T} = T$
{{qed|lemma}}
=== Equality of Relations ===
The [[Definition:Composition of Mappings|composite of $f$ and $I_T$]] is defined as: 
 :$I_T \circ f = \set {	uple {x, z} \in S 	imes T: \exists y \in T: 	uple {x, y} \in f \land 	uple {y, z} \in I_T}$
But by definition of the [[Definition:Identity Mapping|identity mapping]] on $T$, we have that: 
 :$	uple {y, z} \in I_T \implies y = z$
Hence: 
 :$I_T \circ f = \set {	uple {x, y} \in S 	imes T: \exists y \in T: 	uple {x, y} \in f \land 	uple {y, y} \in I_T}$
But as $ orall y \in T: 	uple {y, y} \in I_T$, this means: 
 :$I_T \circ f = \set {	uple {x, y} \in S 	imes T: 	uple {x, y} \in f}$
That is: 
 :$I_T \circ f = f$
{{qed|lemma}}
Hence the result, by [[Equality of Mappings]].
{{qed}}

problem:
Let $S$ and $T$ be [[Definition:Set|sets]].\nLet $f: S \to T$ be a [[Definition:Mapping|mapping]] such that its [[Definition:Inverse of Mapping|inverse]] $f^{-1}: T \to S$ is also a [[Definition:Mapping|mapping]].\nThen: 
 :$\forall x \in S, y \in T: \map f x = y \iff \map {f^{-1} } y = x$
solution_steps:
=== Sufficient Condition ===
Let $f: S 	o T$ be a [[Definition:Mapping|mapping]].
From the definition of [[Definition:Inverse of Mapping|inverse mapping]]: 
 :$f^{-1} = \set {	uple {y, x}: 	uple {x, y} \in f}$
Let $y = f \paren x$.
From the definition of the [[Definition:Preimage of Element under Mapping|preimage of an element]]: 
 :$\map {f^{-1} } y = \set {s \in S: 	uple {y, x} \in f}$
Thus: 
 :$x \in \map {f^{-1} } y$
However, $f^{-1}$ is a [[Definition:Mapping|mapping]].
Therefore, by definition: 
 :$ orall y \in T: 	uple {y, x_1} \in f^{-1} \land 	uple {y, x_2} \in f^{-1} \implies x_1 = x_2$
Thus: 
 :$ orall s \in f^{-1} \paren y: s = x$
Thus: 
 :$\map {f^{-1} } y = \set x$
That is: 
 :$x = \map {f^{-1} } y$
{{qed|lemma}}
=== Necessary Condition ===
Let $\map {f^{-1} } y = x$.
By definition of [[Definition:Inverse Mapping|inverse mapping]]: 
 :$\map f x = y$
{{qed}}

problem: A [[Definition:Mapping|mapping]] $f$ is an [[Definition:Injection|injection]] {{iff}} $f$ is [[Definition:Left Cancellable Mapping|left cancellable]].
solution_steps:
From the definition: a [[Definition:Mapping|mapping]] $f: Y \to Z$ is [[Definition:Left Cancellable Mapping|left cancellable]] {{iff}}: 
 :$\forall X: \forall g_1: X \to Y, g_2: X \to Y: f \circ g_1 = f \circ g_2 \implies g_1 = g_2$\n=== [[Injection iff Left Cancellable/Necessary Condition|Necessary Condition]] === 
 {{:Injection iff Left Cancellable/Necessary Condition}}{{qed|lemma}}\n=== [[Injection iff Left Cancellable/Sufficient Condition|Sufficient Condition]] === 
 {{:Injection iff Left Cancellable/Sufficient Condition}}{{Qed}}

problem: On any [[Definition:Set|set]] $S$, the [[Definition:Identity Mapping|identity mapping]] $I_S: S \to S$ is an [[Definition:Injection|injection]].
solution_steps:
From the definition of the [[Definition:Identity Mapping|identity mapping]]: 
 :$\forall x \in S: \map {I_S} x = x$\nSo: 
 :$\map {I_S} x = \map {I_S} y \implies x = y$\nSo by definition $I_S$ is an [[Definition:Injection|injection]]. 
 {{Qed}}

problem:
A [[Definition:Composition of Mappings|composite]] of [[Definition:Injection|injections]] is an [[Definition:Injection|injection]].\nThat is: 
 :If $f$ and $g$ are [[Definition:Injection|injections]], then so is $f \circ g$.

Let $f$ and $g$ be [[Definition:Mapping|mappings]] such that their [[Definition:Composition of Mappings|composite]] $g \circ f$ is an [[Definition:Injection|injection]].\nThen $f$ is an [[Definition:Injection|injection]].

A [[Definition:Mapping|mapping]] $f: S \to T, S \ne \O$ is an [[Definition:Injection|injection]] {{iff}}: 
 :$\exists g: T \to S: g \circ f = I_S$ 
 where $g$ is a [[Definition:Mapping|mapping]].\nThat is, {{iff}} $f$ has a [[Definition:Left Inverse Mapping|left inverse]].
solution_steps:
Let: 
 :$\exists g: T 	o S: g \circ f = I_S$
From [[Identity Mapping is Injection]], $I_S$ is [[Definition:Injection|injective]], so $g \circ f$ is [[Definition:Injection|injective]].
So from [[Injection if Composite is Injection]], $f$ is an [[Definition:Injection|injection]].
Note that the existence of such a $g$ ''requires'' that $S 
e \O$.
{{qed|lemma}}
Now, assume $f$ is an [[Definition:Injection|injection]].
We now define a [[Definition:Mapping|mapping]] $g: T 	o S$ as follows.
As $S 
e \O$, we choose $x_0 \in S$.
By definition of [[Definition:Injection/Definition 3|injection]]: 
 :$f^{-1} {estriction_{\Img f} } 	o S$ is a [[Definition:Mapping|mapping]] 
 so it is possible to define: 
 :$\map g y =  egin{cases} 
 x_0: & y \in T \setminus \Img f

\map {f^{-1} } y: & y \in \Img f \end{cases}$ 
 :[[File:InjectionIffLeftInverse.png|750px]]
It does not matter what the [[Definition:Element|elements]] of $T \setminus \Img f$ are.
Using the construction given, the equation $g \circ f = I_S$ holds whatever value (or values) we choose for $g \sqbrk {T \setminus \Img f}$.
The remaining [[Definition:Element|elements]] of $T$ can be mapped arbitrarily, and will not affect the [[Definition:Image of Element under Mapping|image]] of any $x \in S$ under the [[Definition:Mapping|mapping]] $g \circ f$.
So, for all $x \in S$: 
 :$\map {g \circ f} x = \map g {\map f x}$ 
 is the [[Definition:Unique|unique]] [[Definition:Element|element]] of $S$ which $f$ maps to $\map f x$.
This unique element is $x$.
Thus $g \circ f = I_S$. 
 {{Qed}}

problem:
Let $S, T$ be [[Definition:Set|sets]] such that $S$ is a [[Definition:Subset|subset]] of $T$.\nThen the [[Definition:Inclusion Mapping|inclusion mapping]] $i_S: S \to T$ defined as: 
 :$\forall x \in S: \map {i_S} x = x$ 
 is an [[Definition:Injection|injection]].\nFor this reason the [[Definition:Inclusion Mapping|inclusion mapping]] can be known as the '''canonical injection of $S$ to $T$'''.
On any set $S$, the [[Definition:Identity Mapping|identity mapping]] $I_S: S \to S$ is a [[Definition:Surjection|surjection]].

Let $f: S \to T$ be a [[Definition:Mapping|mapping]].\nLet $g: S \to \Img f$ be the [[Definition:Restriction of Mapping|restriction]] of $f$ to $S \times \Img f$.\nThen $g$ is a [[Definition:Surjective Restriction|surjective restriction]] of $f$.
solution_steps:
From [[Image is Subset of Codomain]]: 
 :$\Img g \subseteq T$
Furthermore, by definition of [[Definition:Image of Mapping|image]], we have: 
 :$ orall s \in S: g \paren s \in \Img g$
Therefore, $g$ may be viewed as a [[Definition:Mapping|mapping]] $g: S 	o \Img g$.
Thus, by definition, $g$ is a [[Definition:Surjection/Definition 2|surjection]].
{{qed}}

problem:
A [[Definition:Composition of Mappings|composite]] of [[Definition:Surjection|surjections]] is a [[Definition:Surjection|surjection]].\nThat is: 
 :If $g$ and $f$ are [[Definition:Surjection|surjections]], then so is $g \circ f$.
Let $f: S_1 \to S_2$ and $g: S_2 \to S_3$ be [[Definition:Mapping|mappings]] such that $g \circ f$ is a [[Definition:Surjection|surjection]].\nThen $g$ is a [[Definition:Surjection|surjection]].
solution_steps: Let $g \circ f$ be [[Definition:Surjection|surjective]].\nFix $z \in S_3$.

problem:
A [[Definition:Mapping|mapping]] $f: S \to T, S \ne \O$ is a [[Definition:Surjection|surjection]] {{iff}}: 
 :$\exists g: T \to S: f \circ g = I_T$ 
 where: 
 :$g$ is a [[Definition:Mapping|mapping]] 
 :$I_T$ is the [[Definition:Identity Mapping|identity mapping]] on $T$.\nThat is, {{iff}} $f$ has a [[Definition:Right Inverse Mapping|right inverse]].
solution_steps:
Assume $\exists g: T 	o S: f \circ g = I_T$.
From [[Identity Mapping is Surjection]], $I_T$ is [[Definition:Surjection|surjective]], so $f \circ g$ is [[Definition:Surjection|surjective]].
So from [[Surjection if Composite is Surjection]], $f$ is a [[Definition:Surjection|surjection]].
Note that the existence of such a $g$ ''requires'' that $S$ is [[Definition:Non-Empty Set|non-empty]].
Now, assume $f$ is a [[Definition:Surjection|surjection]].
Consider the [[Definition:Indexed Family of Sets|indexed family]] of [[Definition:Non-Empty Set|non-empty]] [[Definition:Set|sets]] $\set {\map {f^{-1} } y}_{y \mathop \in T}$ where $\map {f^{-1} } y$ denotes the [[Definition:Preimage of Element under Mapping|preimage]] of $y$ under $f$.
Using the [[Axiom:Axiom of Choice|axiom of choice]], there exists a [[Definition:Mapping|mapping]] $g: T 	o S$ such that $\map g y \in \set {\map {f^{-1} } y}$ for all $y \in T$. 
 :[[File:SurjectionIffRightInverse.png|550px]]
That is, $\map {\paren {f \circ g} } y = y$, as desired.
{{qed}}

problem: The [[Definition:Identity Mapping|identity mapping]] $I_S: S \to S$ on the [[Definition:Set|set]] $S$ is a [[Definition:Bijection|bijection]].
solution_steps:
The [[Definition:Identity Mapping|identity mapping]] is: 
 :an [[Definition:Injection|injection]], from [[Identity Mapping is Injection]] 
 :a [[Definition:Surjection|surjection]], from [[Identity Mapping is Surjection]] 
 and therefore a [[Definition:Bijection|bijection]].\n{{qed}}

problem: The [[Definition:Identity Mapping|identity mapping]] $I_S: S \to S$ on the [[Definition:Set|set]] $S$ is a [[Definition:Permutation|permutation]].
solution_steps: The identity mapping $I_S$ is a [[Definition:Bijection|bijection]] from $S$ to itself, by [[Identity Mapping is Bijection]].\n{{qed}}

problem:
Let $f: S \to T$ be a [[Definition:Mapping|mapping]]. 
 $f$ is a [[Definition:Bijection|bijection]] {{iff}}: 
 :$(1): \quad \exists g_1: T \to S: g_1 \circ f = I_S$ 
 :$(2): \quad \exists g_2: T \to S: f \circ g_2 = I_T$ 
 where both $g_1$ and $g_2$ are mappings.\nIt also follows that it is necessarily the case that $g_1 = g_2$ for such to be possible.
solution_steps:
=== Necessary Condition ===\nLet $f: S \to T$ be a [[Definition:Mapping|mapping]].\nLet $f$ be such that: 
 :$\exists g_1: T \to S: g_1 \circ f = I_S$ 
 :$\exists g_2: T \to S: f \circ g_2 = I_T$ 
 where both $g_1$ and $g_2$ are [[Definition:Mapping|mappings]].\nThen from [[Left and Right Inverse Mappings Implies Bijection]] it follows that $f$ is a [[Definition:Bijection|bijection]].\nFrom [[Left and Right Inverses of Mapping are Inverse Mapping]] it follows that: 
 :$g_1 = g_2 = f^{-1}$ 
 where $f^{-1}$ is the [[Definition:Inverse Mapping|inverse]] of $f$.\n=== Sufficient Condition ===\nLet $f: S \to T$ be a [[Definition:Bijection|bijection]].\nThen from [[Bijection has Left and Right Inverse]] it follows that: 
 :$f^{-1} \circ f = I_S$ and 
 :$f \circ f^{-1} = I_T$ 
 where $f^{-1}$ is the [[Definition:Inverse Mapping|inverse]] of $f$.

problem:
Let $f: S \to T$ be a [[Definition:Bijection/Definition 1|bijection]] in the sense that: 
 :$(1): \quad f$ is an [[Definition:Injection|injection]] 
 :$(2): \quad f$ is a [[Definition:Surjection|surjection]].\nThen the [[Definition:Inverse of Mapping|inverse]] $f^{-1}$ of $f$ is itself a [[Definition:Bijection/Definition 1|bijection]] by the same definition.
solution_steps:
Let $f$ be both an [[Definition:Injection|injection]] and a [[Definition:Surjection|surjection]].
From [[Mapping is Injection and Surjection iff Inverse is Mapping]] it follows that its [[Definition:Inverse of Mapping|inverse $f^{-1}$]] is a [[Definition:Mapping|mapping]].
From [[Inverse of Inverse Relation]]: 
 : $\paren {f^{-1} }^{-1} = f$
Thus the [[Definition:Inverse of Mapping|inverse of $f^{-1}$]] is $f$.
But then $f$, being a [[Definition:Bijection|bijection]], is by definition a [[Definition:Mapping|mapping]].
So from [[Mapping is Injection and Surjection iff Inverse is Mapping]] it follows that $f^{-1}$ is a [[Definition:Bijection|bijection]].
{{qed}}

problem:
Let $S$ and $T$ be [[Definition:Set|sets]].\nLet $f: S \to T$ be a [[Definition:Bijection|bijection]].\nThen: 
 :$\map {f^{-1} } y = x \iff \map f x = y$ 
 where $f^{-1}$ is the [[Definition:Inverse Mapping|inverse mapping]] of $f$.
solution_steps:
Suppose $f$ is a [[Definition:Bijection|bijection]].
Because $f^{-1}$ is a [[Definition:Bijection|bijection]] from [[Bijection iff Inverse is Bijection]], it is by definition a [[Definition:Mapping|mapping]].
The result follows directly from [[Image of Element under Inverse Mapping]].
{{qed}}

problem:
Let $f: S \to T$ be a [[Definition:Bijection|bijection]].\nThen: 
 : $f^{-1} \circ f = I_S$ 
 : $f \circ f^{-1} = I_T$ 
 where $I_S$ and $I_T$ are the [[Definition:Identity Mapping|identity mappings]] on $S$ and $T$ respectively.

Let $f: S \to T$ be a [[Definition:Bijection|bijection]].\nThen: 
 :$\paren {f^{-1} }^{-1} = f$ 
 where $f^{-1}$ is the [[Definition:Inverse of Mapping|inverse of $f$]].
solution_steps: A [[Definition:Mapping|mapping]] is a [[Definition:Relation|relation]].\nThus it follows that [[Inverse of Inverse Relation]] can be applied directly.\n{{qed}}

problem:
Let $f$ and $g$ be [[Definition:Mapping|mappings]] such that $\Dom f = \Cdm g$.\nThen: 
 :If $f$ and $g$ are both [[Definition:Bijection|bijections]], then so is $f \circ g$ 
 where $f \circ g$ is the [[Definition:Composition of Mappings|composite mapping]] of $f$ with $g$.
solution_steps:
As every [[Definition:Bijection|bijection]] is also by definition an [[Definition:Injection|injection]], a [[Definition:Composition of Mappings|composite]] of [[Definition:Bijection|bijections]] is also a [[Definition:Composition of Mappings|composite]] of [[Definition:Injection|injections]].
Every [[Definition:Composition of Mappings|composite]] of [[Definition:Injection|injections]] is also an [[Definition:Injection|injection]] by [[Composite of Injections is Injection]].
As every [[Definition:Bijection|bijection]] is also by definition a [[Definition:Surjection|surjection]], a [[Definition:Composition of Mappings|composite]] of [[Definition:Bijection|bijections]] is also a [[Definition:Composition of Mappings|composite]] of [[Definition:Surjection|surjections]].
Every [[Definition:Composition of Mappings|composite]] of [[Definition:Surjection|surjections]] is also a [[Definition:Surjection|surjection]] by [[Composite of Surjections is Surjection]].
As a [[Definition:Composition of Mappings|composite]] of [[Definition:Bijection|bijections]] is therefore both an [[Definition:Injection|injection]] and a [[Definition:Surjection|surjection]], it is also a [[Definition:Bijection|bijection]]. 
 {{Qed}}

problem:
Let $f$ and $g$ be [[Definition:Bijection|bijections]] such that $\Dom g = \Cdm f$.\nThen: 
 :$\paren {g \circ f}^{-1} = f^{-1} \circ g^{-1}$ 
 and $f^{-1} \circ g^{-1}$ is itself a [[Definition:Bijection|bijection]].
solution_steps:
$\left({g \circ f}ight)^{-1} = f^{-1} \circ g^{-1}$ is a specific example of [[Inverse of Composite Relation]].
As $f$ and $g$ are [[Definition:Bijection|bijections]] then by [[Bijection iff Inverse is Bijection]], so are both $f^{-1}$ and $g^{-1}$.
By [[Composite of Bijections is Bijection]], it follows that $f^{-1} \circ g^{-1}$ is a [[Definition:Bijection|bijection]].
{{qed}}

problem: Let $f, g$ are [[Definition:Permutation|permutations]] of a [[Definition:Set|set]] $S$.\nThen their [[Definition:Composition of Mappings|composite]] $g \circ f$ is also a [[Definition:Permutation|permutation]] of $S$.
solution_steps: This follows from the fact that a [[Definition:Permutation|permutation]] is a [[Definition:Bijection|bijection]].

\nThe [[Definition:Domain of Mapping|domain]] and [[Definition:Codomain of Mapping|codomain]] are coincident.\nThe result follows from [[Composite of Bijections is Bijection]]. 
 {{Qed}}

problem: If $f$ is a [[Definition:Permutation|permutation]] of $S$, then so is its [[Definition:Inverse of Mapping|inverse]] $f^{-1}$.
solution_steps:
Let $f: S 	o S$ is a [[Definition:Permutation|permutation]] of $S$.
By definition, a [[Definition:Permutation|permutation]] is a [[Definition:Bijection|bijection]] such that the [[Definition:Domain of Mapping|domain]] and [[Definition:Codomain of Mapping|codomain]] are the same [[Definition:Set|set]].
From [[Bijection iff Inverse is Bijection]], it follows $f^{-1}$ is a [[Definition:Bijection|bijection]].
From the definition of [[Definition:Inverse Relation|inverse relation]], the [[Definition:Domain of Mapping|domain]] of a [[Definition:Relation|relation]] is the [[Definition:Codomain of Mapping|codomain]] of its [[Definition:Inverse Relation|inverse]] and vice versa.
Thus the [[Definition:Domain of Mapping|domain]] and [[Definition:Codomain of Mapping|codomain]] of $f^{-1}$ are both $S$ and it follows that $f^{-1}$ is a [[Definition:Permutation|permutation]]. 
 {{Qed}}

problem: Let $f: S \to T$ be an [[Definition:Injection|injection]].\nLet $g: T \to S$ be a [[Definition:Left Inverse Mapping|left inverse]] of $f$.\nThen $g$ is a [[Definition:Surjection|surjection]].
solution_steps:
Let $f: S 	o T$ be an [[Definition:Injection|injection]].
Then from [[Injection iff Left Inverse]] there exists at least one [[Definition:Left Inverse Mapping|left inverse]] $g: T 	o S$ of $f$ such that $g \circ f = I_S$. 
 $I_S$ is a [[Identity Mapping is Surjection|surjection]].
Thus $g \circ f$ is a [[Definition:Surjection|surjection]].
By [[Surjection if Composite is Surjection]] it follows that $g$ is also a [[Definition:Surjection|surjection]]. 
 {{Qed}}

problem: Let $f: S \to T$ be a [[Definition:Mapping|mapping]].\nLet $g: T \to S$ be a [[Definition:Right Inverse Mapping|right inverse]] of $f$.\nThen $g$ is an [[Definition:Injection|injection]].
solution_steps:
By the definition of [[Definition:Right Inverse Mapping|right inverse]]: 
 :$f \circ g = I_T$ 
 where $I_T$ is the [[Definition:Identity Mapping|identity mapping]] on $T$.
By [[Identity Mapping is Injection]], $I_T$ is an [[Definition:Injection|injection]].
By [[Injection if Composite is Injection]], it follows that $g$ is an [[Definition:Injection|injection]].
{{qed}}

problem: [[Definition:Set Equivalence|Set equivalence]] is an [[Definition:Equivalence Relation|equivalence relation]].

Let $S$ and $T$ be [[Definition:Set|sets]] which are [[Definition:Set Equivalence|equivalent]]: 
 :$S \sim T$\nLet $a \in S$ and $b \in T$.\nThen: 
 : $S \setminus \left\{{a}\right\} \sim T \setminus \left\{{b}\right\}$ 
 where $\setminus$ denotes [[Definition:Set Difference|set difference]].
solution_steps:
As $S \sim T$, there exists a [[Definition:Bijection|bijection]] $f: S \to T$.\nLet $g: \left({S \setminus \left\{{a}\right\}}\right) \to \left({T \setminus \left\{{b}\right\}}\right)$ be the [[Definition:Mapping|mapping]] defined as follows: 
 $\forall x \in S \setminus \left\{{a}\right\}: g \left({x}\right) = 
 \begin{cases} 
 f \left({x}\right): f \left({x}\right) \ne b

f \left({a}ight): f \left({x}ight) = b 
 \end{cases}$
First it is shown that $g$ is an [[Definition:Injection|injection]].
Let $x, y \in S \setminus \left\{{a}ight\}$ and $x 
e y$.
The following cases apply: 
 $(1): \quad f \left({x}ight) 
e b$ and $f \left({y}ight) 
e b$
By definition of $g$: 
 :$f \left({x}ight) = g \left({x}ight)$ 
 and: 
 :$f \left({y}ight) = g \left({y}ight)$
It follows from the [[Definition:Injection|injectivity]] of $f$ that: 
 :$g \left({x}ight) 
e g \left({y}ight)$ 
 $(2): \quad f \left({x}ight) = b$
By definition of $g$: 
 :$g \left({x}ight) = f \left({a}ight)$
We have that: 
 :$y \in S \setminus \left\{{a}ight\}$
Therefore by definition of [[Definition:Set Difference|set difference]]: 
 :$y 
e a$
So by the [[Definition:Injection|injectivity]] of $f$: 
 :$f \left({y}ight) 
e f \left({a}ight) = g \left({x}ight)$
Also by the [[Definition:Injection|injectivity]] of $f$: 
 :$f \left({y}ight) 
e b$
Thus, by the definition of $g$: 
 :$g \left({y}ight) = f \left({y}ight) 
e f \left({a}ight) = g \left({x}ight)$ 
 $(2): \quad f \left({y}ight) = b$
The proof is the same as that of case $2$, with the roles of $x$ and $y$ reversed.
{{qed|lemma}}
Next it is shown that $g$ is a [[Definition:Surjection|surjection]].
Let $y \in T \setminus \left\{{b}ight\}$.
By definition of [[Definition:Surjection|surjectivity]] of $f$: 
 :$\exists x \in S: f \left({x}ight) = y$
It is to be shown that: 
 :$\exists x' \in S, x 
e a: f \left({x'}ight) = y$ 
 $(1): \quad f \left({a}ight) = b$ 
 [[Definition:By Hypothesis|By hypothesis]]: 
 :$f \left({x}ight) = y 
e b$
Then by the [[Rule of Transposition|rule of transposition]]: 
 :$x 
e a$ 
 so let: 
 :$x' = x$ 
 $(2): \quad f \left({a}ight) 
e b$
By the [[Rule of Transposition|rule of transposition]]: 
 :$f^{-1} \left({b}ight) 
e a$ 
 where $f^{-1} \left({b}ight)$ denotes the [[Definition:Preimage of Element under Mapping|preimage]] of $b$ under $f$. 
 :Case $(2.1): \quad f \left({a}ight) = y$
By the definition of $g$: 
 :$g \left({f^{-1} \left({b}ight)}ight) = y$ 
 so let: 
 :$x' = f^{-1} \left({b}ight)$ 
 :Case $(2.2): \quad f \left({a}ight) 
e y$ 
 [[Definition:By Hypothesis|By hypothesis]]: 
 :$f \left({x}ight) = y 
e f \left({a}ight)$
Then by the [[Rule of Transposition|rule of transposition]]: 
 :$x 
e a$ 
 so let: 
 :$x' = x$
Thus: 
 :$ orall y \in T \setminus \left\{{b}ight\}: \exists x \in S \setminus \left\{{a}ight\}: g \left({x}ight) = y$ 
 and so $g$ is a [[Definition:Surjection|surjection]].
{{qed|lemma}}
Thus $g$ is both an [[Definition:Injection|injection]] and a [[Definition:Surjection|surjection]], and is by definition a [[Definition:Bijection|bijection]].
{{qed}}

problem:
Let $S$ and $T$ be [[Definition:Set|sets]].
Let $f: S 	o T$ be a [[Definition:Mapping|mapping]].
Let $A$ and $B$ be [[Definition:Subset|subsets]] of $S$.
Then: 
 :$f \sqbrk {A \cup B} = f \sqbrk A \cup f \sqbrk B$
This can be expressed in the language and notation of [[Definition:Direct Image Mapping|direct image mappings]] as: 
 :$ orall A, B \in \powerset S: \map {f^	o} {A \cup B} = \map {f^	o} A \cup \map {f^	o} B$
solution_steps:
As $f$, being a [[Definition:Mapping|mapping]], is also a [[Definition:Relation|relation]], we can apply [[Image of Union under Relation]]: 
 :$\RR \sqbrk {A \cup B} = \RR \sqbrk A \cup \RR \sqbrk B$\n{{qed}}

problem:
Let $S$ and $T$ be [[Definition:Set|sets]].
Let $f: S 	o T$ be a [[Definition:Mapping|mapping]].
Let $S_1$ and $S_2$ be [[Definition:Subset|subsets]] of $S$.
Then: 
 :$f \sqbrk {S_1 \cap S_2} \subseteq f \sqbrk {S_1} \cap f \sqbrk {S_2}$
This can be expressed in the language and notation of [[Definition:Direct Image Mapping|direct image mappings]] as: 
 :$ orall S_1, S_2 \in \powerset S: \map {f^	o} {S_1 \cap S_2} \subseteq \map {f^	o} {S_1} \cap \map {f^	o} {S_2}$
solution_steps:
As $f$, being a [[Definition:Mapping|mapping]], is also a [[Definition:Relation|relation]], we can apply [[Image of Intersection under Relation]]: 
 :$\RR \sqbrk {S_1 \cap S_2} \subseteq \RR \sqbrk {S_1} \cap \RR \sqbrk {S_2}$\n{{qed}}

problem:
Let $S$ and $T$ be [[Definition:Set|sets]].\nLet $f: S \to T$ be a [[Definition:Mapping|mapping]].\nThen: 
 :$\forall A, B \subseteq S: f \sqbrk {A \cap B} = f \sqbrk A \cap f \sqbrk B$ 
 {{iff}} $f$ is an [[Definition:Injection|injection]].
solution_steps:
An [[Definition:Injection|injection]] is a type of [[Definition:One-to-One Relation|one-to-one relation]], and therefore also a [[Definition:One-to-Many Relation|one-to-many relation]].
Therefore [[Image of Intersection under One-to-Many Relation]] applies: 
 :$ orall A, B \subseteq S: \mathcal R \left[{A \cap B}ight] = \mathcal R \left[{A}ight] \cap \mathcal R \left[{B}ight]$ 
 {{iff}} $\mathcal R$ is a [[Definition:One-to-Many Relation|one-to-many relation]].
We have that $f$ is a [[Definition:Mapping|mapping]] and therefore a [[Definition:Many-to-One Relation|many-to-one relation]].
So $f$ is a [[Definition:One-to-Many Relation|one-to-many relation]] {{iff}} $f$ is also an [[Definition:Injection|injection]].
It follows that: 
 :$ orall A, B \subseteq S: f \left[{A \cap B}ight] = f \left[{A}ight] \cap f \left[{B}ight]$ 
 {{iff}} $f$ is an [[Definition:Injection|injection]].
{{qed}}

problem:
Let $S$ and $T$ be [[Definition:Set|sets]].\nLet $\RR \subseteq S \times T$ be a [[Definition:Relation|relation]].\nThen: 
 :$\forall S_1, S_2 \subseteq S: \RR \sqbrk {S_1 \cap S_2} = \RR \sqbrk {S_1} \cap \RR \sqbrk {S_2}$ 
 {{iff}} $\RR$ is [[Definition:One-to-Many Relation|one-to-many]].

Let $\mathcal R \subseteq S \times T$ be a [[Definition:Relation|relation]].\nLet $A$ and $B$ be [[Definition:Subset|subsets]] of $S$.\nThen: 
 :$(1): \quad \mathcal R \left[{A}\right] \setminus \mathcal R \left[{B}\right] = \mathcal R \left[{A \setminus B}\right]$ 
 {{iff}} $\mathcal R$ is [[Definition:One-to-Many Relation|one-to-many]].
solution_steps:
=== Sufficient Condition ===
First, to show that $(1)$ holds if $\mathcal R$ is [[Definition:One-to-Many Relation|one-to-many]].
From [[Image of Set Difference under Relation]], we already have: 
 :$\mathcal R \left[{A}ight] \setminus \mathcal R \left[{B}ight] \subseteq \mathcal R \left[{A \setminus B}ight]$
So we just need to show: 
 :$\mathcal R \left[{A \setminus B}ight] \subseteq \mathcal R \left[{A}ight] \setminus \mathcal R \left[{B}ight]$
Let $t 
otin \mathcal R \left[{A}ight] \setminus \mathcal R \left[{B}ight]$.
Then $t 
otin \mathcal R \left[{A}ight] \lor t \in \mathcal R \left[{B}ight]$ by [[De Morgan's Laws (Logic)|De Morgan's Laws]]. 
 {{explain|Go into more detail}}
Suppose $t 
otin \mathcal R \left[{A}ight]$.
Then by definition of a [[Definition:Relation|relation]]: 
 : $
eg \exists s \in A: \left({s, t}ight) \in \mathcal R$
By [[Image of Subset is Subset of Image]]: 
 : $\mathcal R \left[{A \setminus B}ight] \subseteq \mathcal R \left[{A}ight]$
Thus, by [[Definition:Subset|definition of subset]] and [[Rule of Transposition]]: 
 : $t 
otin \mathcal R \left[{A}ight] \implies t 
otin \mathcal R \left[{A \setminus B}ight]$
Now suppose $t \in \mathcal R \left[{B}ight]$.
Then: 
 : $\exists s \in B: \left({s, t}ight) \in \mathcal R$
Because $\mathcal R$ is [[Definition:One-to-Many Relation|one-to-many]]: 
 : $ orall x \in S: \left({x, t}ight) \in \mathcal R \implies x = s$ 
 and thus: 
 : $x \in B$
Thus: 
 : $x 
otin A \setminus B$ 
 and hence: 
 : $t 
otin \mathcal R \left[{A \setminus B}ight]$
So by [[Proof by Cases]]: 
 : $t 
otin \mathcal R \left[{A}ight] \setminus \mathcal R \left[{B}ight] \implies t 
otin \mathcal R \left[{A \setminus B}ight]$
The result follows from [[Set Complement inverts Subsets]]: 
 :$S \subseteq T \iff \complement \left({T}ight) \subseteq \complement \left({S}ight)$
{{qed|lemma}}
=== Necessary Condition ===
Now for the converse: If $(1)$ holds, it is to be shown that $\mathcal R$ is [[Definition:One-to-Many Relation|one-to-many]].
Let $s, t \in S$ be [[Definition:Distinct Elements|distinct]].
That is, $s 
e t$.
Then in particular: 
 : $\left\{{s}ight\} \setminus \left\{{t}ight\} = \left\{{s}ight\}$
Applying $(1)$ to these two sets, it follows that: 
 :$\mathcal R \left[{\left\{{s}ight\}}ight] \setminus \mathcal R \left[{\left\{{t}ight\}}ight] = \mathcal R \left[{\left\{{s}ight\}}ight]$
By [[Set Difference with Disjoint Set]], this implies that: 
 :$\mathcal R \left[{\left\{{s}ight\}}ight] \cap \mathcal R \left[{\left\{{t}ight\}}ight] = \varnothing$
It follows that every element of $T$ can be related to at most one element of $S$.
That is, $\mathcal R$ is [[Definition:One-to-Many Relation|one-to-many]].
{{qed}} 
 [[Category:Set Difference]] 
 [[Category:Relation Theory]]

problem:
Let $S$ and $T$ be [[Definition:Set|sets]].
Let $f: S 	o T$ be a [[Definition:Mapping|mapping]].
Let $T_1$ and $T_2$ be [[Definition:Subset|subsets]] of $T$.
Then: 
 :$f^{-1} \sqbrk {T_1 \cup T_2} = f^{-1} \sqbrk {T_1} \cup f^{-1} \sqbrk {T_2}$
This can be expressed in the language and notation of [[Definition:Inverse Image Mapping|inverse image mappings]] as: 
 :$ orall T_1, T_2 \in \powerset T: \map {f^\gets} {T_1 \cup T_2} = \map {f^\gets} {T_1} \cup \map {f^\gets} {T_2}$
solution_steps:
As $f$, being a [[Definition:Mapping|mapping]], is also a [[Definition:Relation|relation]], we can apply [[Preimage of Union under Relation]]: 
 :$\RR^{-1} \sqbrk {T_1 \cup T_2} = \RR^{-1} \sqbrk {T_1} \cup \RR^{-1} \sqbrk {T_2}$\n{{qed}}

problem:
Let $f: S 	o T$ be a [[Definition:Mapping|mapping]].
Let $T_1$ and $T_2$ be [[Definition:Subset|subsets]] of $T$.
Then: 
 :$f^{-1} \sqbrk {T_1 \cap T_2} = f^{-1} \sqbrk {T_1} \cap f^{-1} \sqbrk {T_2}$
This can be expressed in the language and notation of [[Definition:Inverse Image Mapping|inverse image mappings]] as: 
 :$ orall T_1, T_2 \in \powerset T: \map {f^\gets} {T_1 \cap T_2} = \map {f^\gets} {T_1} \cap \map {f^\gets} {T_2}$
solution_steps:
As $f$, being a [[Definition:Mapping|mapping]], is also a [[Definition:Many-to-One Relation|many-to-one relation]], it follows from [[Inverse of Many-to-One Relation is One-to-Many]] that its [[Definition:Inverse of Mapping|inverse]] $f^{-1}$ is a [[Definition:One-to-Many Relation|one-to-many relation]].\nThus [[Image of Intersection under One-to-Many Relation]] applies: 
 :$\mathcal R \sqbrk {T_1 \cap T_2} = \mathcal R \sqbrk {T_1} \cap \mathcal R \sqbrk {T_2}$ 
 where here $\mathcal R = f^{-1}$. 
 {{Qed}}

problem:
Let $f: S \to T$ be a [[Definition:Mapping|mapping]].\nLet $T_1$ and $T_2$ be [[Definition:Subset|subsets]] of $T$.\nThen: 
 :$f^{-1} \sqbrk {T_1 \setminus T_2} = f^{-1} \sqbrk {T_1} \setminus f^{-1} \sqbrk {T_2}$ 
 where: 
 :$\setminus$ denotes [[Definition:Set Difference|set difference]] 
 :$f^{-1} \sqbrk {T_1}$ denotes [[Definition:Preimage of Subset under Mapping|preimage]].

Let $S$ and $T$ be [[Definition:Non-Empty Set|non-empty]] [[Definition:Set|sets]].
Let $S 	imes T$ be the [[Definition:Cartesian Product|Cartesian product]] of $S$ and $T$.
Let $\pr_1: S 	imes T 	o T$ and $\pr_2: S 	imes T 	o T$ be the [[Definition:First Projection|first projection]] and [[Definition:Second Projection|second projection]] respectively on $S 	imes T$.
Then $\pr_1$ and $\pr_2$ are both [[Definition:Surjection|surjections]].
solution_steps:
Let $S$ and $T$ be [[Definition:Set|sets]] such that neither is [[Definition:Empty Set|empty]].
Let $\pr_1: S 	imes T 	o S$ be the [[Definition:First Projection|first projection]] on $S 	imes T$.
Then by definition of [[Definition:First Projection|first projection]]: 
 :$ orall x \in S: \exists 	uple {x, t} \in S 	imes T: \map {\pr_1} {x, t} = x$
Similarly, let $\pr_2: S 	imes T 	o T$ be the [[Definition:Second Projection|second projection]] on $S 	imes T$.
Then by definition of [[Definition:Second Projection|second projection]]: 
 :$ orall x \in T: \exists 	uple {s, x} \in S 	imes T: \map {\pr_2} {s, x} = x$
Hence the result.
{{qed}}

problem:
Let $\map f x, \map j x, \map k x$ be [[Definition:Real Function|real functions]] defined on the [[Definition:Open Real Interval|open interval]] $I$.
Let $\xi \in I$ be a point in $I$ at which both $j$ and $k$ are [[Definition:Differentiable Real Function at Point|differentiable]].
Let $\map f x = \map j x + \map k x$.
Then $f$ is [[Definition:Differentiable Real Function at Point|differentiable]] at $\xi$ and: 
 :$\map {f'} \xi = \map {j'} \xi + \map {k'} \xi$
It follows from the definition of [[Definition:Derivative on Interval|derivative]] that if $j$ and $k$ are both [[Definition:Differentiable on Interval|differentiable]] on the interval $I$, then: 
 :$ orall x \in I: \map {f'} x = \map {j'} x + \map {k'} x$
solution_steps: It can be observed that this is an example of a [[Linear Combination of Derivatives]] with $\lambda = \mu = 1$.\n{{qed}}

problem: Let $\RR$ be an [[Definition:Equivalence Relation|equivalence relation]] on $S$.\nThen the [[Definition:Quotient Mapping|quotient mapping]] $q_\RR: S \to S / \RR$ is a [[Definition:Surjection|surjection]].

Let $\Delta_S$ be the [[Definition:Diagonal Relation|diagonal relation]] on a [[Definition:Set|set]] $S$.\nLet $q_{\Delta_S}: S \to S / \Delta_S$ be the [[Definition:Trivial Quotient|trivial quotient of $S$]].\nThen $q_{\Delta_S}: S \to S / \Delta_S$ is a [[Definition:Bijection|bijection]].
solution_steps:
The [[Definition:Diagonal Relation|diagonal relation]] is defined as: 
 :$\Delta_S = \set {	uple {x, x}: x \in S} \subseteq S 	imes S$
From the fact that $q_{\Delta_S}$ is a [[Definition:Quotient Mapping|quotient mapping]], we know that it is a [[Quotient Mapping is Surjection|surjection]].
It relates each $x \in S$ to the [[Definition:Singleton|singleton]] $\set x$.
Thus: 
 :$\set x = \eqclass x {\Delta_S} \subseteq S$
So $\map {q_{\Delta_S} } x = \set x$, and it follows that: 
 :$\eqclass x {\Delta_S} = \eqclass y {\Delta_S} \implies x = y$
Thus $q_{\Delta_S}$ is [[Definition:Injection|injective]], and therefore by definition a [[Definition:Bijection|bijection]]. 
 {{Qed}}

problem:
Let $f: S \to T$ be a [[Definition:Mapping|mapping]].\nLet $r: S / \RR_f \to \Img f$ be the [[Definition:Renaming Mapping|renaming mapping]], defined as: 
 :$r: S / \RR_f \to \Img f: \map r {\eqclass x {\RR_f} } = \map f x$ 
 where: 
 :$\RR_f$ is the [[Definition:Equivalence Relation Induced by Mapping|equivalence induced]] by the [[Definition:Mapping|mapping]] $f$ 
 :$S / \RR_f$ is the [[Definition:Quotient Set|quotient set]] of $S$ determined by $\RR_f$ 
 :$\eqclass x {\RR_f}$ is the [[Definition:Equivalence Class|equivalence class]] of $x$ under $\RR_f$.\nThe renaming mapping is always [[Definition:Well-Defined Mapping|well-defined]].

Let $f: S \to T$ be a [[Definition:Mapping|mapping]].\nLet $r: S / \RR_f \to \Img f$ be the [[Definition:Renaming Mapping|renaming mapping]], defined as: 
 :$r: S / \RR_f \to \Img f: \map r {\eqclass x {\RR_f} } = \map f x$ 
 where: 
 :$\RR_f$ is the [[Definition:Equivalence Relation Induced by Mapping|equivalence induced]] by the [[Definition:Mapping|mapping]] $f$ 
 :$S / \RR_f$ is the [[Definition:Quotient Set|quotient set]] of $S$ determined by $\RR_f$ 
 :$\eqclass x {\RR_f}$ is the [[Definition:Equivalence Class|equivalence class]] of $x$ under $\RR_f$.\nThe [[Definition:Renaming Mapping|renaming mapping]] is a [[Definition:Bijection|bijection]].
solution_steps:
From [[Renaming Mapping is Well-Defined]], $r: S / \RR_f 	o \Img f$ is a [[Definition:Well-Defined Mapping|well-defined mapping]].
By definition, $\RR_f$ is the [[Definition:Equivalence Relation Induced by Mapping|equivalence relation induced]] by the [[Definition:Mapping|mapping]] $f$.
Hence by definition: 
 :$	uple {s_1, s_2} \in \RR_f \iff \map f {s_1} = \map f {s_2}$
From [[Condition for Mapping from Quotient Set to be Injection]], this is precisely the condition required for $r$ to be an [[Definition:Injection|injection]].
Next it is noted that the [[Definition:Codomain of Mapping|codomain]] of $r$ is $\Img f$.
Then from [[Restriction of Mapping to Image is Surjection]], we have that $f_{estriction \Img f}$ is a [[Definition:Surjection|surjection]].
Hence from [[Condition for Mapping from Quotient Set to be Surjection]] it follows that $r$ is also a [[Definition:Surjection|surjection]].
Thus $r$ is shown to be both an [[Definition:Injection|injection]] and a [[Definition:Surjection|surjection]], and so by definition is a [[Definition:Bijection|bijection]].
{{qed}}

problem:
Let $f: S \to T$ be a [[Definition:Mapping|mapping]].\nThen: 
 :$\forall x \in S: \map {f^{-1} \circ f} x = \eqclass x {\RR_f}$ 
 where: 
 :$\RR_f$ is the [[Definition:Equivalence Relation Induced by Mapping|equivalence induced by $f$]] 
 :$\eqclass x {\RR_f}$ is the [[Definition:Equivalence Class|$\RR_f$-equivalence class of $x$]].
solution_steps:
Let $y = \map f x$.
Then by the definition of [[Definition:Equivalence Relation Induced by Mapping|induced equivalence]]: 
 :$x \in \eqclass x {\RR_f}$
By the definition of the [[Definition:Inverse of Mapping|inverse of a mapping]]: 
 :$f^{-1} = \set {	uple {y, x}: 	uple {x, y} \in f}$
Thus: 
 :$\eqclass x {\RR_f} = \set {s \in \Dom f: \map f s = \map f x}$
By definition: 
 :$\map {f^{-1} } y = \eqclass x {\RR_f}$
Hence the result.
{{qed}}

From [[Surjection by Restriction of Codomain]], any $f: S 	o \Img f$ is a [[Definition:Surjection|surjection]].
The [[Definition:Mapping|mapping]] $g: S 	o \Img f$ where $\map g x = \map f x$ is therefore also clearly a [[Definition:Surjection|surjection]].
The [[Definition:Mapping|mapping]] $g: S 	o \Img f: \map g x = \map f x$ is clearly unique, by [[Equality of Mappings]].
From [[Inclusion Mapping is Injection]], $i_T: \Img f 	o T$ is an [[Definition:Injection|injection]].
Likewise, the mapping $i_T: \Img f 	o T : \map {i_T} x = x$ is also unique, by its own definition.
{{qed}}

From the definition of [[Definition:Equivalence Relation Induced by Mapping|Induced Equivalence]], the [[Definition:Mapping|mapping]] $f: S 	o T$ induces an [[Definition:Equivalence Relation|equivalence]] $\RR_f$ on its [[Definition:Domain of Mapping|domain]].
As $f: S 	o T$ is a [[Definition:Surjection|surjection]], $T = \Img f$ by definition.
From [[Renaming Mapping is Bijection]], the [[Definition:Renaming Mapping|renaming mapping]] $r: S / \RR_f 	o T$ is a [[Definition:Bijection|bijection]], where $S / \RR_f$ is the [[Definition:Quotient Set|quotient set]] of $S$ by $\RR_f$.
Hence: 
 :$r \circ q_{\RR_f} = f$ 
 :$r$ is the only mapping $r: S / \RR_f 	o T$ that satisfies this equality.
{{qed}}
\nS / \RR_f \ar[rrr]_*{r} & & & \Img f \ar[uuu]_*{i_T} } \end {xy}$\n{{qed}}

problem:
The [[Definition:Empty Set|empty set]] is an [[Definition:Element|element]] of all [[Definition:Power Set|power sets]]: 
 :$\forall S: \O \in \powerset S$

Let $S$ and $T$ be [[Definition:Set|sets]].
Let $\mathcal R \subseteq S 	imes T$ be a [[Definition:Relation|relation]] on $S 	imes T$.
Let $\mathcal R^	o: \powerset S 	o \powerset T$ be the [[Definition:Direct Image Mapping of Relation|direct image mapping]] of $\mathcal R$: 
 :$ orall X \in \powerset S: \map {\mathcal R^	o} X = \set {t \in T: \exists s \in X: 	uple {s, t} \in \mathcal R}$
Then $\mathcal R^	o$ is indeed a [[Definition:Mapping|mapping]].
solution_steps:
Take the general relation $\mathcal R \subseteq S 	imes T$.
Let $X \subseteq S$, that is $X \in \powerset S$.
Suppose $X = \O$.
Then from [[Image of Empty Set is Empty Set]]: 
 :$\map {\mathcal R^	o} X = \O \subseteq T$
Suppose $X = S$.
Then from [[Image is Subset of Codomain/Corollary 1|Image is Subset of Codomain: Corollary 1]]: 
 :$\map {\mathcal R^	o} X = \Img {\mathcal R} \subseteq T$
Finally, suppose $\O \subset X \subset S$.
From [[Image is Subset of Codomain]], again: 
 :$\map {\mathcal R^	o} X \subseteq T$
Now, from the definition of the [[Definition:Power Set|power set]], we have that: 
 :$Y \subseteq T \iff Y \in \powerset T$
We defined $R^	o \subseteq \powerset S 	imes \powerset T$ such that: 
 :$R^	o: \powerset S 	o \powerset T: \map {\mathcal R^	o} X = \set {t \in T: \exists s \in X: 	uple {s, t} \in \mathcal R}$
By definition, there is only one $\map {\mathcal R^	o} X$ for any given $X$, and so $R^	o$ is [[Definition:Many-to-One Relation|many-to-one]].
We have shown that: 
 :$ orall X \subseteq S: \map {\mathcal R^	o} X \in \powerset T$
So: 
 :$ orall X \in \powerset S: \exists_1 Y \in \powerset T: \map {\mathcal R^	o} X = Y$
So: 
 :$R^	o$ is defined for all $X \in \powerset S$ 
 and therefore $R^	o$ is a [[Definition:Mapping|mapping]].
{{qed}}

problem:
Let $S$ and $T$ be [[Definition:Set|sets]].
Let $\mathcal R \subseteq S 	imes T$ be a [[Definition:Relation|relation]] on $S 	imes T$.
Let $\mathcal R^\gets$ be the [[Definition:Inverse Image Mapping of Relation|inverse image mapping]] of $\mathcal R$: 
 :$\mathcal R^\gets: \powerset T 	o \powerset S: \map {\mathcal R^\gets} Y = \mathcal R^{-1} \sqbrk Y$
Then $\mathcal R^\gets$ is indeed a [[Definition:Mapping|mapping]].
solution_steps: $\mathcal R^{-1}$, being a [[Definition:Relation|relation]], obeys the same laws as $\mathcal R$.\nSo [[Direct Image Mapping of Relation is Mapping]] applies directly.\n{{qed}}

problem:
Let $f: S \to T$ be an [[Definition:Injection|injection]].\nThen the [[Definition:Direct Image Mapping of Mapping|direct image mapping]] of $f$: 
 :$f^\to: \powerset S \to \powerset T$ 
 is an [[Definition:Injection|injection]].
solution_steps:
Suppose $f^	o: \powerset S 	o \powerset T$ is not an [[Definition:Injection|injection]].
Then: 
 :$\exists Y \in \powerset T: \exists X_1, X_2 \in \powerset S: X_1 
e X_2 \land \map {f^	o} {X_1} = \map {f^	o} {X_2} = Y$
There are two cases to consider: 
 :$(1): \quad$ Either $X_1$ or $X_2$ is the [[Definition:Empty Set|empty set]] 
 :$(2): \quad$ Neither $X_1$ nor $X_2$ is the [[Definition:Empty Set|empty set]].
=== Either subset is the empty set === 
 {{WLOG}} assume that $X_1 = \O$.
Then, from [[Image of Empty Set is Empty Set]]: 
 :$Y = \map {f^	o} {X_1} = \O$
But that means: 
 :$\map {f^	o} {X_2} = \O$
Now $X_1 
e X_2$, so $X_2 
e \O$.
Thus: 
 :$\exists x_2 \in X_2: 
eg \exists y \in T: \map f {x_2} = y$ 
 which means that $f$ is not even a [[Definition:Mapping|mapping]], let alone an [[Definition:Injection|injection]].
The same argument applies if $X_2$ is [[Definition:Empty Set|empty]].
{{qed|lemma}}
=== Neither subset is the empty set ===
Now, assume that neither $X_1$ nor $X_2$ is the [[Definition:Empty Set|empty set]].
As $X_1 
e X_2$, there must be at least one [[Definition:Element|element]] in either one which is not in the other. 
 {{WLOG}} assume that $\exists x_1 \in X_1: x_1 
otin X_2$.
Now suppose $\map f {x_1} = y \in Y$.
However, as $\map {f^	o} {X_2} = Y$, there must be some $x_2 \in X_2$ such that $\map f {x_2} = y \in Y$.
So: 
 :$\exists x_1 
e x_2 \in S: \map f {x_1} = \map f {x_2}$ 
 which means, by definition, that $f$ is not [[Definition:Injection|injective]].
Thus, if $f^	o: \powerset S 	o \powerset T$ is not an [[Definition:Injection|injection]] then neither is $f: S 	o T$.
Thus, by the [[Rule of Transposition]], the result follows.
{{qed}}

problem:
Let $f: S \to T$ be a [[Definition:Surjection|surjection]].\nThen the [[Definition:Direct Image Mapping of Mapping|direct image mapping]] of $f$: 
 :$f^\to: \powerset S \to \powerset T$ 
 is a [[Definition:Surjection|surjection]].
solution_steps:
Let $f^\gets$ be the [[Definition:Inverse Image Mapping of Mapping|inverse image mapping]] of $f$.
Let $Y \in \powerset T$.
Let $X = \map {f^\gets} Y$.
By [[Subset equals Image of Preimage iff Mapping is Surjection]]: 
 :$\map {f^	o} X = Y$
As such an $X$ exists for each $Y \in \powerset S$, $f^	o$ is [[Definition:Surjection|surjective]].
{{qed}}

problem: There is no [[Definition:Surjection|surjection]] from a [[Definition:Set|set]] $S$ to its [[Definition:Power Set|power set]] for any set $S$.\nThat is, $S$ is [[Definition:Strictly Smaller Set|strictly smaller]] than its [[Definition:Power Set|power set]].
solution_steps:
Let $S$ be a [[Definition:Set|set]].
Let $\mathcal P \left({S}ight)$ be the [[Definition:Power Set|power set]] of $S$.
Let $f: S 	o \mathcal P \left({S}ight)$ be a [[Definition:Mapping|mapping]].
Let $T = \left\{{x \in S: 
eg \left({x \in f \left({x}ight)}ight)}ight\}$.
Then $T \subseteq S$, so $T \in \mathcal P \left({S}ight)$ by the definition of [[Definition:Power Set|power set]].
We will show that $T$ is not in the [[Definition:Image of Mapping|image]] of $f$ and therefore $f$ is not [[Definition:Surjection|surjective]].
Aiming for a [[Proof by Contradiction|contradiction]], suppose that: 
 : $\exists a \in S: T = f \left({a}ight)$
Suppose that: 
 : $a \in f \left({a}ight)$
Then by the definition of $T$: 
 : $
eg \left({a \in T}ight)$
Thus since $T = f \left({a}ight)$: 
 : $
eg \left({a \in f \left({a}ight) }ight)$
By [[Rule of Implication]]: 
 : $(1) \quad a \in f \left({a}ight) \implies 
eg \left({ a \in f \left({a}ight) }ight)$
Suppose instead that: 
 : $
eg \left({a \in f \left({a}ight)}ight)$
Then by the definition of $T$: 
 : $a \in T$
Thus since $T = f \left({a}ight)$: 
 : $a \in f \left({a}ight)$
By [[Rule of Implication]]: 
 : $(2) \quad 
eg \left({ a \in f \left({a}ight) }ight) \implies a \in f \left({a}ight)$
By [[Non-Equivalence of Proposition and Negation/Formulation 1|Non-Equivalence of Proposition and Negation]], applied to $(1)$ and $(2)$, this is a [[Definition:Contradiction|contradiction]].
As the specific choice of $a$ did not matter, we derive a contradiction by [[Existential Instantiation]].
Thus by [[Proof by Contradiction]], the supposition that $\exists a \in S: T = f \left({a}ight)$ must be false.
It follows that $f$ is not a [[Definition:Surjection|surjection]].
{{qed}}

problem:
Let $\mathcal R \subseteq S 	imes T$ be a [[Definition:Relation|relation]].
Let $\mathcal R^	o: \powerset S 	o \powerset T$ be the [[Definition:Direct Image Mapping of Relation|direct image mapping]] $\mathcal R$.
Let $\mathcal R^	o$ be a [[Definition:Surjection|surjection]].
Let $X = \Preimg {\mathcal R}$, that is, the [[Definition:Preimage of Relation|preimage]] of $\mathcal R$.
Then $\mathcal R {estriction_X} \subseteq X 	imes T$, that is, the [[Definition:Restriction of Relation|restriction of $\mathcal R$ to $X$]], is a [[Definition:Surjection|surjection]].
solution_steps:
Let $X$ be the [[Definition:Preimage of Relation|preimage]] of $\mathcal R$.
Suppose $\mathcal R {estriction_X} \subseteq X 	imes T$ is a [[Definition:Mapping|mapping]], but not a [[Definition:Surjection|surjection]].
Then: 
 :$\exists y \in T: 
eg \exists x \in S: 	uple {x, y} \in \mathcal R$
Because no [[Definition:Element|element]] of $S$ relates to $y$, no [[Definition:Subset|subset]] of $S$ contains any [[Definition:Element|element]] of $S$ that relates to the [[Definition:Subset|subset]] $\left\{{y}ight\} \subseteq T$.
Thus: 
 :$\exists \set y \in \powerset T: 
eg \exists X \in \powerset S: \map {\mathcal R^	o} X = \set y$
So we see that $\mathcal R^	o: \powerset S 	o \powerset T$ is not a [[Definition:Surjection|surjection]].
Now, suppose $\mathcal R {estriction_X} \subseteq X 	imes T$ is not even a [[Definition:Mapping|mapping]].
This could happen, according to the definition of a [[Definition:Mapping|mapping]], in one of two ways: 
 :$(1): \quad \exists x \in X: 
eg \exists y \in T: 	uple {x, y} \in \mathcal R$ 
 :$(2): \quad \exists x \in S: 	uple {x, y_1} \in \mathcal R \land 	uple {x, y_2} \in \mathcal R: y_1 
e y_2$
Because $X$ is already the [[Definition:Preimage of Relation|preimage of $\mathcal R$]], the first of these cannot happen here.
For the second, it can be seen that neither $\set {y_1}$ nor $\set {y_2}$ can be in $\Rng {\map {\mathcal R^	o} {\powerset S} }$.
Therefore $\mathcal R^	o: \powerset S 	o \powerset T$ can not be a [[Definition:Surjection|surjection]]. 
 {{questionable|See [[Talk:Surjection Induced by Powerset is Induced by Surjection|talk page]]}}
Thus, by the [[Rule of Transposition]], the result follows.
{{qed}}

 [[Category:Direct Image Mappings]]

problem:
Let $f: S 	o T$ be a [[Definition:Mapping|mapping]].
Let $f^	o: \powerset S 	o \powerset T$ be the [[Definition:Direct Image Mapping of Mapping|direct image mapping]] of $f$.
Let $f^	o$ be an [[Definition:Injection|injection]].
Then $f: S 	o T$ is also an [[Definition:Injection|injection]].
solution_steps:
Suppose $f: S \to T$ is a [[Definition:Mapping|mapping]], but not [[Definition:Injection|injective]].\nThen: 
 : $\exists x_1 \ne x_2 \in S: \map f {x_1} = \map f {x_2} = y$\nLet: 
 :$X_1 = \set {x_1}$ 
 :$X_2 = \set {x_2}$ 
 :$Y = \set y$\nThen it follows that: 
 :$\map {f^\to} {X_1} = \map {f^\to} {X_2} = Y$\nThus $f^\to: \powerset S \to \powerset T$ is not [[Definition:Injection|injective]].\nSo by the [[Rule of Transposition]], the result follows.\n{{qed}}

problem: Let $S$ be a [[Definition:Set|set]].\nLet $\powerset S$ denote the [[Definition:Power Set|power set]] of $S$.\nThere is no [[Definition:Bijection|bijection]] $f: S \to \powerset S$.
solution_steps:
A [[Definition:Bijection|bijection]] is by its definition also a [[Definition:Surjection|surjection]].\nBy [[Cantor's Theorem]] there is no [[Definition:Surjection|surjection]] from $S$ to $\powerset S$.\nHence the result. 
 {{Qed}}

problem:
If a [[Definition:Subset|subset]] of one [[Definition:Set|set]] is [[Definition:Set Equivalence|equivalent]] to the other, and a [[Definition:Subset|subset]] of the other is [[Definition:Set Equivalence|equivalent]] to the first, then the two [[Definition:Set|sets]] are themselves [[Definition:Set Equivalence|equivalent]]: 
 :$\forall S, T: T \sim S_1 \subseteq S \land S \sim T_1 \subseteq T \implies S \sim T$
solution_steps:

 \end {cases}$
From the above, we have that: 
 :$elcomp S {\mathbb G} \subseteq g \sqbrk T$
Therefore, as $g$ is an [[Definition:Injection|injection]], it follows that the [[Definition:Preimage of Element under Mapping|preimage]] $\map {g^{-1} } x$ is a [[Definition:Singleton|singleton]].
So $h$ is a [[Definition:Bijection|bijection]] by dint of the [[Definition:Injection|injective]] nature of both $f$ and $g^{-1}$.
{{qed}}

problem: Let $S$ be a [[Definition:Set|set]].\nLet $\RR$ be the [[Definition:Trivial Ordering|trivial ordering]] on $S$.\nThen $\RR$ is [[Definition:Universally Compatible Relation|universally compatible]].
solution_steps:
To prove that the trivial ordering is in fact an ordering, we need to checking each of the criteria for an [[Definition:Ordering|ordering]]:
=== Reflexivity === 
 :$ orall a \in S: a \mathrel \RR a$:
From its definition, we have $ orall a, b \in S: a = b \implies a \mathrel \RR b$.
Thus, as $a = a$, we have $ orall a \in S: a \mathrel \RR a$.
So [[Definition:Reflexive Relation|reflexivity]] is proved.
=== Transitivity === 
 :$ orall a, b, c \in S: a \mathrel \RR b \land b \mathrel \RR c \implies a \mathrel \RR c$:
From the definition: 
 :$a \mathrel \RR b \iff a = b$ 
 :$b \mathrel \RR c \iff b = c$
So as $a = b \land b = c \implies a = c$ from [[Equality is Equivalence Relation|transitivity of equals]], we have that $a \mathrel \RR c$ and thus [[Definition:Transitive Relation|transitivity]] is proved.
=== Antisymmetry === 
 :$ orall a, b \in S: a \mathrel \RR b \land b \mathrel \RR a \implies a = b$:
From the definition: 
 :$a \mathrel \RR b \iff a = b$ 
 :$b \mathrel \RR a \iff b = a$ 
 [[Definition:Antisymmetric Relation|Antisymmetry]] follows from [[Equality is Equivalence Relation|symmetry of equals]].
The [[Definition:Trivial Ordering|trivial ordering]] is by definition the same as the [[Definition:Diagonal Relation|diagonal relation]], and is therefore [[Diagonal Relation is Universally Compatible|universally compatible]].
{{qed}}

problem: Let $\left({S, \preceq}\right)$ be an [[Definition:Ordered Set|ordered set]].\nThe [[Definition:Identity Mapping|identity mapping]] $I_S$ is an [[Definition:Order Isomorphism|order isomorphism]] from $\left({S, \preceq}\right)$ to itself.
solution_steps:
By definition: 
 :$\forall x \in S: I_S \left({x}\right) = x$\nSo: 
 :$x \preceq y \implies I_S \left({x}\right) \preceq I_S \left({y}\right)$\nAs $I_S$ is a [[Identity Mapping is Bijection|bijection]], we also have: 
 :$I_S^{-1} \left({x}\right) = x$\nSo: 
 :$x \preceq y \implies I_S^{-1} \left({x}\right) \preceq I_S^{-1} \left({y}\right)$\n{{qed}}

problem:
Let $\left({S, \preceq_1}\right)$ and $\left({T, \preceq_2}\right)$ be [[Definition:Ordered Set|ordered sets]].\nLet $\phi$ be a [[Definition:Bijection|bijection]] from $\left({S, \preceq_1}\right)$ to $\left({T, \preceq_2}\right)$.\nThen: 
 : $\phi: \left({S, \preceq_1}\right) \to \left({T, \preceq_2}\right)$ 
 is an [[Definition:Order Isomorphism|order isomorphism]] {{iff}}: 
 : $\phi^{-1}: \left({T, \preceq_2}\right) \to \left({S, \preceq_1}\right)$ 
 is also an [[Definition:Order Isomorphism|order isomorphism]].
solution_steps:
Follows directly from the definition of [[Definition:Order Isomorphism|order isomorphism]].\nLet $\phi: \left({S, \preceq_1}\right) \to \left({T, \preceq_2}\right)$ be an [[Definition:Order Isomorphism|order isomorphism]].\nThen: 
 :$\forall x, y, \in S: x \preceq_1 y \implies \phi \left({x}\right) \preceq_2 \phi \left({y}\right)$ 
 and: 
 :$\forall \phi \left({x}\right), \phi \left({y}\right) \in T: \phi \left({x}\right) \preceq_2 \phi \left({y}\right) \implies \phi^{-1} \left({\phi \left({x}\right)}\right) \preceq_1 \phi^{-1} \left({\phi \left({y}\right)}\right)$\nThat is: 
 :$\forall \phi \left({x}\right), \phi \left({y}\right) \in T: \phi \left({x}\right) \preceq_2 \phi \left({y}\right) \implies x \preceq_1 y$\nHence the result. 
 {{Qed}}

problem:
Let $\left({S_1, \preceq_1}\right)$, $\left({S_2, \preceq_2}\right)$ and $\left({S_3, \preceq_3}\right)$ be [[Definition:Ordered Set|ordered sets]].\nLet: 
 : $\phi: \left({S_1, \preceq_1}\right) \to \left({S_2, \preceq_2}\right)$ 
 and: 
 : $\psi: \left({S_2, \preceq_2}\right) \to \left({S_3, \preceq_3}\right)$ 
 be [[Definition:Order Isomorphism|order isomorphisms]].\nThen $\psi \circ \phi: \left({S_1, \preceq_1}\right) \to \left({S_3, \preceq_3}\right)$ is also an [[Definition:Order Isomorphism|order isomorphism]].
solution_steps:
From [[Composite of Bijections is Bijection]], $\psi \circ \phi$ is a [[Definition:Bijection|bijection]], as, by definition, an [[Definition:Order Isomorphism|order isomorphism]] is also a [[Definition:Bijection|bijection]].
By definition of [[Definition:Composition of Mappings|composition of mappings]], $\psi \circ \phi \left({x}ight) = \psi \left({\phi \left({x}ight)}ight)$.
As $\phi$ is an [[Definition:Order Isomorphism|order isomorphism]], we have: 
 :$ orall x_1, y_1 \in S_1: x_1 \preceq_1 y_1 \implies \phi \left({x_1}ight) \preceq_2 \phi \left({y_1}ight)$
As $\psi$ is an [[Definition:Order Isomorphism|order isomorphism]], we have: 
 :$ orall x_2, y_2 \in S_2: x_2 \preceq_2 y_2 \implies \psi \left({x_2}ight) \preceq_3 \psi \left({y_2}ight)$
By setting $x_2 = \phi \left({x_1}ight), y_2 = \phi \left({y_1}ight)$, it follows that: 
 :$ orall x_1, y_1 \in S_1: x_1 \preceq_1 y_1 \implies \psi \left({\phi \left({x_1}ight)}ight) \preceq_3 \psi \left({\phi \left({y_1}ight)}ight)$
Similarly we can show that: 
 :$ orall x_3, y_3 \in S_3: x_3 \preceq_3 y_3 \implies \phi^{-1} \left({\psi^{-1} \left({x_3}ight)}ight) \preceq_1 \phi^{-1} \left({\psi^{-1} \left({y_3}ight)}ight)$
{{qed}}

problem: Let $\left({S, \preceq}\right)$ be an [[Definition:Ordered Set|ordered set]].\nLet $\succeq$ denote the [[Definition:Dual Ordering|dual ordering]] of $\preceq$.\nThen $\succeq$ is an [[Definition:Ordering|ordering]] on $S$.
solution_steps:
By definition, $\succeq$ is the [[Definition:Inverse Relation|inverse relation]] to $\preceq$.
By [[Inverse Relation Properties]], if a [[Definition:Relation|relation]] is [[Definition:Reflexive Relation|reflexive]], [[Definition:Transitive Relation|transitive]] and/or [[Definition:Antisymmetric Relation|antisymmetric]], then so is its [[Definition:Inverse Relation|inverse]].
The result follows.
{{qed}}

problem: Let $S$ be a [[Definition:Set|set]].\nLet $\left({\mathcal P \left({S}\right), \subseteq}\right)$ be the [[Definition:Relational Structure|relational structure]] defined on $\mathcal P \left({S}\right)$ by the [[Definition:Relation|relation]] $\subseteq$.\nThen $\left({\mathcal P \left({S}\right), \subseteq}\right)$ is a [[Definition:Complete Lattice|complete lattice]].
solution_steps:
From [[Subset Relation on Power Set is Partial Ordering]], we have that $\subseteq$ is a [[Definition:Partial Ordering|partial ordering]].
We note in passing that for any set $S$: 
 : From [[Supremum of Power Set]], $\mathcal P \left({S}ight)$ has a [[Definition:Supremum of Set|supremum]], that is, $S$ itself 
 : From [[Infimum of Power Set]], $\mathcal P \left({S}ight)$ has an [[Definition:Infimum of Set|infimum]], that is, $\varnothing$.
These are also the [[Definition:Maximal Element|maximal]] and [[Definition:Minimal Element|minimal]] elements of $\mathcal P \left({S}ight)$.
Let $\mathbb S$ be a [[Definition:Subset|subset]] of $\mathcal P \left({S}ight)$.
Then from [[Union is Smallest Superset]]: 
 :$\left({ orall X \in \mathbb S: X \subseteq T}ight) \iff  igcup \mathbb S \subseteq T$ 
 and from [[Intersection is Largest Subset]]: 
 :$\left({ orall X \in \mathbb S: T \subseteq X}ight) \iff T \subseteq  igcap \mathbb S$
So $ igcap \mathbb S$ is the [[Definition:Infimum of Set|infimum]] and $ igcup \mathbb S$ is the [[Definition:Supremum of Set|supremum]] of $\left({\mathbb S, \subseteq}ight)$.
Hence by definition $\mathcal P \left({S}ight)$ is a [[Definition:Complete Lattice|complete lattice]].
{{qed}}

problem: Every [[Definition:Totally Ordered Set|totally ordered set]] is a [[Definition:Lattice|lattice]].
solution_steps:
Let $\left({S, \preceq}\right)$ be a [[Definition:Totally Ordered Set|totally ordered set]].\nThen we have: 
 : $\forall x, y \in S: x \preceq y \lor y \preceq x$ 
 : $\forall x, y \in S: x \preceq y \implies \sup \left\{{x, y}\right\} = y \land \inf \left\{{x, y}\right\} = x$ 
 : $\forall x, y \in S: y \preceq x \implies \sup \left\{{x, y}\right\} = x \land \inf \left\{{x, y}\right\} = y$\nThus the conditions for $\left({S, \preceq}\right)$ to be a [[Definition:Lattice|lattice]] are fulfilled.\n{{qed}}

problem:
Let $\struct {S, \preceq}$ be an [[Definition:Ordered Set|ordered set]].
Then $\preceq$ is a [[Definition:Total Ordering|total ordering]] {{iff}}: 
 :$ orall a, b \in S: \paren {a \prec b} \lor \paren {a = b} \lor \paren {a \succ b}$
That is, every element either [[Definition:Strictly Precede|strictly precedes]], is [[Definition:Equals|the same as]], or [[Definition:Strictly Succeed|strictly succeeds]], every other element.
In other words, {{iff}} $\prec$ is a [[Definition:Trichotomy|trichotomy]].
If $r$ is rational $(r \neq 0)$ and $x$ is irrational, prove that $rx$ is irrational.
solution_steps: \begin{proof}
If $r x$ were rational, then $x=\frac{r x}{r}$ would also be rational.
\end{proof}

problem: Let $E$ be a nonempty subset of an ordered set; suppose $\alpha$ is a lower bound of $E$ and $\beta$ is an upper bound of $E$. Prove that $\alpha \leq \beta$.
solution_steps: \begin{proof}

Since $E$ is nonempty, there exists $x \in E$. Then by definition of lower and upper bounds we have $\alpha \leq x \leq \beta$, and hence by property $i i$ in the definition of an ordering, we have $\alpha<\beta$ unless $\alpha=x=\beta$.
\end{proof}

problem: Prove that no order can be defined in the complex field that turns it into an ordered field.
solution_steps: \begin{proof}

By Part (a) of Proposition $1.18$, either $i$ or $-i$ must be positive. Hence $-1=i^2=(-i)^2$ must be positive. But then $1=(-1)^2$, must also be positive, and this contradicts Part $(a)$ of Proposition 1.18, since 1 and $-1$ cannot both be positive.
\end{proof}

problem: If $z_1, \ldots, z_n$ are complex, prove that $|z_1 + z_2 + \ldots + z_n| \leq |z_1| + |z_2| + \cdots + |z_n|$.
solution_steps: \begin{proof}
We can apply the case $n=2$ and induction on $n$ to get
\begin{aligned}
\left|z_1+z_2+\cdots z_n\right| &=\left|\left(z_1+z_2+\cdots+z_{n-1}\right)+z_n\right|
& \leq\left|z_1+z_2+\cdots+z_{n-1}\right|+\left|z_n\right|
& \leq\left|z_1\right|+\left|z_2\right|+\cdots+\left|z_{n-1}\right|+\left|z_n\right|
\end{aligned}
\end{proof}

problem: If $z$ is a complex number such that $|z|=1$, that is, such that $z \bar{z}=1$, compute $|1+z|^{2}+|1-z|^{2}$.
solution_steps: \begin{proof}
$|1+z|^2=(1+z)(1+\bar{z})=1+\bar{z}+z+z \bar{z}=2+z+\bar{z}$. Similarly $|1-z|^2=(1-z)(1-\bar{z})=1-z-\bar{z}+z \bar{z}=2-z-\bar{z}$. Hence
\end{proof}

problem: Prove that $|\mathbf{x}+\mathbf{y}|^{2}+|\mathbf{x}-\mathbf{y}|^{2}=2|\mathbf{x}|^{2}+2|\mathbf{y}|^{2}$ if $\mathbf{x} \in R^{k}$ and $\mathbf{y} \in R^{k}$.
solution_steps: \begin{proof}
The proof is a routine computation, using the relation
|x \pm y|^2=(x \pm y) \cdot(x \pm y)=|x|^2 \pm 2 x \cdot y+|y|^2 .

If $\mathrm{x}$ and $\mathrm{y}$ are the sides of a parallelogram, then $\mathrm{x}+\mathrm{y}$ and $\mathbf{x}-\mathrm{y}$ are its diagonals. Hence this result says that the sum of the squares on the diagonals of a parallelogram equals the sum of the squares on the sides.
\end{proof}

problem: If $k = 1$ and $\mathbf{x} \in R^{k}$, prove that there does not exist $\mathbf{y} \in R^{k}$ such that $\mathbf{y} \neq 0$ but $\mathbf{x} \cdot \mathbf{y}=0$
solution_steps: \begin{proof}
Not true when $k=1$, since the product of two nonzero real numbers is nonzero.
\end{proof}

problem: If $A$ and $B$ are disjoint closed sets in some metric space $X$, prove that they are separated.
solution_steps: \begin{proof}
We are given that $A \cap B=\varnothing$. Since $A$ and $B$ are closed, this means $A \cap \bar{B}=\varnothing=\bar{A} \cap B$, which says that $A$ and $B$ are separated.
\end{proof}

problem: Prove that every compact metric space $K$ has a countable base.
solution_steps: \begin{proof}
$K$ can be covered by a finite union of neighborhoods of radius $1 / n$, and this shows that this implies that $K$ is separable.

It is not entirely obvious that a metric space with a countable base is separable. To prove this, let $\left\{V_n\right\}_{n=1}^{\infty}$ be a countable base, and let $x_n \in V_n$. The points $V_n$ must be dense in $X$. For if $G$ is any non-empty open set, then $G$ contains $V_n$ for some $n$, and hence $x_n \in G$. (Thus for a metric space, having a countable base and being separable are equivalent.)
\end{proof}

problem: Suppose $E\subset\mathbb{R}^k$ is uncountable, and let $P$ be the set of condensation points of $E$. Prove that at most countably many points of $E$ are not in $P$.
solution_steps: \begin{proof}

If $x \in W^c$, and $O$ is any neighborhood of $x$, then $x \in V_n \subseteq O$ for some n. Since $x \notin W, V_n \cap E$ is uncountable. Hence $O$ contains uncountably many points of $E$, and so $x$ is a condensation point of $E$. Thus $x \in P$, i.e., $W^c \subseteq P$.

Conversely if $x \in W$, then $x \in V_n$ for some $V_n$ such that $V_n \cap E$ is countable. Hence $x$ has a neighborhood (any neighborhood contained in $V_n$ ) containing at most a countable set of points of $E$, and so $x \notin P$, i.e., $W \subseteq P^c$. Hence $P=W^c$.

It is clear that $P$ is closed (since its complement $W$ is open), so that we need only show that $P \subseteq P^{\prime}$. Hence suppose $x \in P$, and $O$ is any neighborhood of $x$. (By definition of $P$ this means $O \cap E$ is uncountable.) We need to show that there is a point $y \in P \cap(O \backslash\{x\})$. If this is not the case, i.e., if every point $y$ in $O \backslash\{x\}$ is in $P^c$, then for each such point $y$ there is a set $V_n$ containing $y$ such that $V_n \cap E$ is at most countable. That would mean that $y \in W$, i.e., that $O \backslash\{x\}$ is contained in $W$. It would follow that $O \cap E \subseteq\{x\} \cup(W \cap E)$, and so $O \cap E$ contains at most a countable set of points, contrary to the hypothesis that $x \in P$. Hence $O$ contains a point of $P$ different from $x$, and so $P \subseteq P^{\prime}$. Thus $P$ is perfect.
\end{proof}

problem: Prove that every open set in $\mathbb{R}$ is the union of an at most countable collection of disjoint segments.
solution_steps: \begin{proof}

Let $O$ be open. For each pair of points $x \in O, y \in O$, we define an equivalence relation $x \sim y$ by saying $x \sim y$ if and only if $[\min (x, y), \max (x, y)] \subset$ 0 . This is an equivalence relation, since $x \sim x([x, x] \subset O$ if $x \in O)$; if $x \sim y$, then $y \sim x$ (since $\min (x, y)=\min (y, x)$ and $\max (x, y)=\max (y, x))$; and if $x \sim y$ and $y \sim z$, then $x \sim z([\min (x, z), \max (x, z)] \subseteq[\min (x, y), \max (x, y)] \cup$ $[\min (y, z), \max (y, z)] \subseteq O)$. In fact it is easy to prove that
\min (x, z) \geq \min (\min (x, y), \min (y, z))
and
\max (x, z) \leq \max (\max (x, y), \max (y, z))
It follows that $O$ can be written as a disjoint union of pairwise disjoint equivalence classes. We claim that each equivalence class is an open interval.

To show this, for each $x \in O$; let $A=\{z:[z, x] \subseteq O\}$ and $B=\{z:[x, z] \subseteq$ $O\}$, and let $a=\inf A, b=\sup B$. We claim that $(a, b) \subset O$.
